section.title: The Seventy-Two and the Good Samaritan
section.sectionRationale: As the journey to Jerusalem unfolds, Jesus widens the mission by sending seventy-two ahead of Him, then teaches them to rejoice not in power but in grace. He praises the Father for revealing the kingdom to little children, answers a lawyer's test with the parable of the Good Samaritan, and ends in a home where Mary chooses the one necessary thing. Mission, mercy, and attentive love to the Lord belong together.
section.thesis: The kingdom advances through humble sent messengers who rejoice that their names are written in heaven, it bends down in costly mercy to the half-dead stranger, and it is received above all by those who sit at Jesus' feet and choose the one thing needful.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the sending and warning of the seventy-two; their joyful return and Jesus' thanksgiving; the parable of the Good Samaritan; and Martha and Mary and the one necessary thing.

verses.text: After this, the Lord appointed seventy-two others and sent them two by two ahead of Him to every town and place He was about to visit.
And He told them, “The harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few. Ask the Lord of the harvest, therefore, to send out workers into His harvest.
Go! I am sending you out like lambs among wolves.
Carry no purse or bag or sandals. Do not greet anyone along the road.
Whatever house you enter, begin by saying, ‘Peace to this house.’
If a man of peace is there, your peace will rest on him; if not, it will return to you.
Stay at the same house, eating and drinking whatever you are offered. For the worker is worthy of his wages. Do not move around from house to house.
If you enter a town and they welcome you, eat whatever is set before you.
Heal the sick who are there and tell them, ‘The kingdom of God is near you.’
But if you enter a town and they do not welcome you, go into the streets and declare,
‘Even the dust of your town that clings to our feet, we wipe off as a testimony against you. Yet be sure of this: The kingdom of God is near.’
I tell you, it will be more bearable on that day for Sodom than for that town.
Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! For if the miracles that were performed in you had been performed in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes.
But it will be more bearable for Tyre and Sidon at the judgment than for you.
And you, Capernaum, will you be lifted up to heaven? No, you will be brought down to Hades!
Whoever listens to you listens to Me; whoever rejects you rejects Me; and whoever rejects Me rejects the One who sent Me.”

units.label: The joyful return and Jesus' thanksgiving
units.note: rejoice that your names are written in heaven

verses.text: The seventy-two returned with {{chara|joy}} and said, “Lord, even the demons submit to us in Your name.”
So He told them, “I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven.
Behold, I have given you authority to tread on snakes and scorpions, and over all the power of the enemy. Nothing will harm you.
Nevertheless, do not rejoice that the spirits submit to you, but rejoice that your names are written in heaven.”

At that time Jesus rejoiced in the Holy Spirit and declared, “I praise You, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because You have hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children. Yes, Father, for this was well-pleasing in Your sight.
All things have been entrusted to Me by My Father. No one knows who the Son is except the Father, and no one knows who the Father is except the Son and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal Him.”
Then Jesus turned to the disciples and said privately, “Blessed are the eyes that see what you see.
For I tell you that many prophets and kings desired to see what you see but did not see it, and to hear what you hear but did not hear it.”
One day an expert in the law stood up to test Him. “Teacher,” he asked, “what must I do to inherit eternal life?”
“What is written in the Law?” Jesus replied. “How do you read it?”
He answered, “‘{{agapao|Love}} the Lord your God with all your heart and with all your soul and with all your strength and with all your mind’ and ‘Love your {{plesion|neighbor}} as yourself.’”
“You have answered correctly,” Jesus said. “Do this and you will live.”
But wanting to justify himself, he asked Jesus, “And who is my {{plesion|neighbor}}?”
Jesus took up this question and said, “A man was going down from Jerusalem to Jericho when he fell into the hands of robbers. They stripped him, beat him, and went away, leaving him half dead.
Now by chance a priest was going down the same road, but when he saw him, he passed by on the other side.
So too, when a Levite came to that spot and saw him, he passed by on the other side.
But a Samaritan on a journey came upon him, and when he saw him, he {{splanchnizomai|had compassion}}.
He went to him and bandaged his wounds, pouring on oil and wine. Then he put him on his own animal, brought him to an inn, and took care of him.
The next day he took out two denarii and gave them to the innkeeper. ‘Take care of him,’ he said, ‘and on my return I will repay you for any additional expense.’
Which of these three do you think was a {{plesion|neighbor}} to the man who fell into the hands of robbers?”
“The one who showed him mercy,” replied the expert in the law. Then Jesus told him, “Go and do likewise.”
As they traveled along, Jesus entered a village where a woman named Martha welcomed Him into her home.
She had a sister named Mary, who sat at the Lord’s feet listening to His message.
But Martha was distracted by all the preparations to be made. She came to Jesus and said, “Lord, do You not care that my sister has left me to serve alone? Tell her to help me!”
“Martha, Martha,” the Lord replied, “you are worried and upset about many things.
But only one thing is necessary. Mary has chosen the good portion, and it will not be taken away from her.”

chara.gloss: Joy, gladness. The seventy-two return with joy over their authority, but Jesus redirects the deeper joy to a surer ground: that their names are written in heaven. Gospel joy rests finally on grace received, not on results achieved.

agapao.gloss: To love with the will and in action, to seek another's good. The great commandment gathers up the whole law: to love God wholly and the neighbor as oneself. The parable then shows that such love is measured not in words but in costly mercy.

plesion.gloss: Neighbor, the one near at hand. The lawyer tries to narrow who counts as neighbor; Jesus reverses the question, so that being a neighbor means becoming one to whoever lies in need, even a despised stranger.

splanchnizomai.gloss: To be moved with compassion in the depths, a gut-level mercy. The same verb describes Jesus' own heart for the crowds and the father's welcome of the prodigal. Here it moves the Samaritan to cross the road and pay the price of another's rescue.

basic.context: On the way to Jerusalem Jesus sends out seventy-two workers ahead of Him with the message that the kingdom of God is near. They return thrilled that even demons obeyed, and Jesus teaches them to rejoice instead that their names are written in heaven. He thanks the Father for revealing the kingdom to little children. Then a lawyer tests Him about eternal life, and Jesus answers his self-justifying question, who is my neighbor, with the story of a Samaritan who shows mercy where a priest and a Levite would not. Finally, in Martha's home, Mary sits at Jesus' feet, and Jesus says she has chosen the one thing that cannot be taken away.
basic.hermeneutics: Hold the three scenes together. The mission is urgent but its true joy is grace, not success. The parable is not a tidy lesson in being nice; it explodes the lawyer's attempt to limit love and demands mercy toward the outsider. And Martha and Mary guard the whole against activism: even good service must flow from sitting at Jesus' feet and hearing His word.
basic.originalLanguages: The seventy-two return with chara, joy, which Jesus anchors in heaven rather than in power. The lawyer cites the command to agapao, to love, God and neighbor, plesion. The turning point of the parable is splanchnizomai, gut-deep compassion, the very mercy that moves the heart of God.
basic.typology: The Samaritan who finds the half-dead man, binds his wounds, pays his debt, and promises to return has long been heard as an image of Christ Himself, who comes to the ruined and rescues at His own cost. The good portion Mary chooses is the Word made flesh, present and speaking.
basic.christ: Jesus is the sender of the harvest workers and the one in whom the kingdom draws near. He is the Son who alone knows and reveals the Father. In the parable He both commands mercy and embodies it, the true neighbor who crosses every barrier to save. And He is the one necessary thing at whose feet Mary sits.
basic.god: The Father is Lord of heaven and earth, who hides the kingdom from the self-sufficient and reveals it to little children according to His good pleasure. He is the God whose mercy the parable mirrors and whose Son is the harvest's Lord.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 19 and Deuteronomy 6 supply the great commandment. Matthew 9 shows Jesus moved with the same compassion for the harvest crowds. James 2 insists that love without deeds is dead. Philippians 3 counts all things loss for the one thing of knowing Christ.
basic.oneStory: The kingdom advances through humble sent workers, bends down in costly mercy to the outsider, and is received by those who sit and listen at Jesus' feet. In the merciful Samaritan we glimpse the Lord who rescues us, and in Mary we see the heart that treasures Him above all.

context.literary: This chapter sits early in Luke's travel narrative. The sending of the seventy-two widens the mission of the Twelve in chapter 9 and points beyond Israel to the nations. The Good Samaritan and the Martha and Mary scene are found only in Luke, showcasing his special interest in mercy, outsiders, and the place of women at Jesus' feet.
context.historical: Seventy-two, or seventy in some manuscripts, may echo the seventy nations of Genesis 10, signaling a mission destined for the world. Jews and Samaritans were bitter enemies, so a Samaritan hero would have jolted the lawyer. The road from Jerusalem down to Jericho was a notoriously dangerous descent through wilderness.
context.canonical: The command to love God and neighbor stands at the heart of the law, quoted from Deuteronomy 6:5 and Leviticus 19:18. Jesus' claim that the Son alone reveals the Father anticipates the high Christology of John's Gospel. The parable becomes a lasting definition of neighbor love in Christian ethics.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To commission the wider mission of the kingdom, to anchor the disciples' joy in grace rather than power, to shatter every attempt to limit the command to love, and to hold active service under the priority of listening to Jesus.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The specific instructions to the seventy-two describe one mission moment, not a rulebook for all travel. What abides is the priority of the harvest, the ground of joy in salvation, the command of boundary-crossing mercy, and the necessity of sitting at Jesus' feet.

debates.question: Is the Good Samaritan mainly a call to love or a picture of Christ?

views.label: A call to merciful love
views.case: In its setting the parable answers who is my neighbor and ends with go and do likewise, pressing every hearer to costly mercy toward whoever is in need, even an enemy.

views.label: A picture of Christ the rescuer
views.case: Many read the Samaritan, the despised outsider who saves the half-dead man at his own cost and promises to return, as an image of Jesus who comes to the ruined and pays for their healing.

debates.bearing: The primary thrust is the command to show mercy, yet the mercy commanded is precisely the mercy Christ shows us, so the two readings reinforce rather than cancel each other.

debates.question: Does the praise of Mary devalue Martha's service?

views.label: Listening must come first
views.case: Jesus gently corrects not Martha's serving but her anxious distraction; Mary has chosen the one necessary thing, hearing His word, from which true service flows.

views.label: A rebuke of hospitality
views.case: Some hear it as downgrading practical service, but the context, a welcomed guest and an anxious host, shows the issue is worried, self-important busyness that crowds out attention to the Lord.

debates.bearing: Service is good, but it must be rooted in and never replace attentive love to Jesus; Mary's good portion is the wellspring, not the enemy, of faithful work.

study.originalLanguages: The seventy-two come back with chara, joy, at their power over demons, and Jesus lifts their joy to firmer ground: rejoice that your names are written in heaven. The lawyer rightly quotes the command to agapao, to love, the Lord with all one's being and the plesion, the neighbor, as oneself, but he tries to shrink the circle of neighbor. Jesus answers with splanchnizomai, the gut-deep compassion that moves the Samaritan across the road, the very mercy that marks the heart of God and of His Son. Finally Mary, at the Lord's feet, chooses the good portion that will not be taken from her.

typology.type: The Samaritan who rescues the half-dead man at his own cost
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the despised and rejected one, who comes to us in our ruin, binds our wounds, pays our debt, and promises to return, Isaiah 53:3 and Luke 19:10.

typology.type: The seventy-two sent to every town
typology.fulfillment: The mission of the church to all nations, gathering the harvest until the Lord of the harvest returns, Matthew 28:18-20.

typology.type: Mary's good portion at Jesus' feet
typology.fulfillment: The one thing needful, knowing Christ, that surpasses all else and cannot be taken away, Philippians 3:8.

study.christ: Jesus is at the center of every scene. He is the Lord of the harvest who sends and the One before whom towns stand or fall by their welcome. He is the Son who alone knows the Father and freely reveals Him to whom He wills. In the parable He commands the mercy He Himself supremely shows, the true neighbor who crosses the deepest barrier, from heaven to earth, to save the helpless at infinite cost and to return for them. And in Martha's home He is the one necessary thing, the Word to be heard, the good portion Mary has chosen and will never lose.
study.god: The Father is Lord of heaven and earth, sovereign in revelation, hiding the kingdom from the wise and self-sufficient and unveiling it to little children because it pleased Him to do so. His good pleasure, not human cleverness, opens eyes to the Son. The compassion that fills the parable reflects His own heart, and the joy He commands, that our names are written in heaven, rests on His gracious election and welcome. The whole passage displays a God who saves the lowly, the outsider, and the listening heart.

crossReferences.note: Love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, and strength, the first half of the great commandment the lawyer cites.
Love your neighbor as yourself, the command the parable expounds and enlarges.
Jesus moved with compassion for the harassed crowds, calling for workers for the plentiful harvest.
Counting everything loss for the surpassing worth of knowing Christ, the one thing Mary chooses.

study.oneStory: As Jesus journeys toward the cross, the kingdom goes out through humble sent messengers, reaches down in costly mercy to the half-dead outsider, and is treasured by those who sit at His feet. The merciful Samaritan mirrors the Lord who rescues us at His own expense and promises to return, and Mary's good portion is the Christ who is Himself the one necessary thing. Mission, mercy, and attentive love to Jesus are woven into one seamless call.

sermon.title: The Good Samaritan: On Love
sermon.why: Keller expounds the parable of the Good Samaritan from Luke 10, showing that love is what we were built for and that Jesus is the ultimate neighbor who rescues us at His own cost. A faithful, Christ-centered handling of this movement's central parable.

quiz.q: Why did Jesus tell the seventy-two to rejoice?

options.text: That their names are written in heaven, rather than merely that demons submitted to them.
options.note: Luke 10:20 redirects their joy from power to their secure standing in grace.

options.text: That they had authority to tread on snakes and scorpions.
options.note: He did grant this authority, but told them the deeper joy is their names in heaven, not their power.

options.text: That they had become more important than other disciples.
options.note: The point is grace, not status; nothing here elevates them over others.

options.text: That they would be spared all future hardship.
options.note: He sends them as lambs among wolves; the promise is heavenly security, not an easy life.

quiz.q: What was the lawyer trying to do when he asked, who is my neighbor?

options.text: To justify himself by narrowing whom he was obligated to love.
options.note: Luke 10:29 says he asked wanting to justify himself.

options.text: To genuinely learn the limits of the command out of humble curiosity.
options.note: The wording sounds like a sincere question, but the text says he was seeking to justify himself.

options.text: To trap Jesus into denying the law of Moses.
options.note: He came to test Jesus, but this particular question aimed at self-justification, not at contradicting the law.

options.text: To confess that he had failed to love his neighbor.
options.note: The opposite: he was defending himself, not confessing failure.

quiz.q: What moved the Samaritan to help the wounded man?

options.text: He had compassion, a gut-deep mercy, and acted at his own cost.
options.note: Luke 10:33-35 shows compassion leading to costly, practical care.

options.text: He bandaged the man's wounds and paid the innkeeper.
options.note: True actions, but they flowed from the compassion that first moved him; the deeds are the fruit, the mercy the root.

options.text: He felt obligated because the man was a fellow Samaritan.
options.note: The wounded man was a Jew; the Samaritan crossed enmity, not kinship, to help.

options.text: He hoped to be repaid and honored for his trouble.
options.note: He paid out of his own pocket and promised to cover more, seeking no reward.

quiz.q: What did Jesus mean by the one thing necessary that Mary had chosen?

options.text: Sitting at His feet to hear His word, the good portion that cannot be taken away.
options.note: Luke 10:42 commends Mary's attentive listening as the one necessary thing.

options.text: That quiet is better than serving guests.
options.note: It is not stillness in general but hearing Jesus that is praised; service is good when it flows from that.

options.text: That Martha was wrong to welcome Jesus into her home.
options.note: Martha's welcome was good; her anxious distraction, not her hospitality, was the problem.

options.text: That practical work does not matter to Jesus at all.
options.note: Jesus values service, but it must not crowd out listening to Him.

quiz.q: How does the Good Samaritan point to Christ?

options.text: As the despised outsider who rescues the helpless at His own cost and promises to return, He mirrors Jesus who saves us and will come again.
options.note: The mercy commanded is the very mercy Christ shows in coming to save the ruined.

options.text: He shows that we should be kind to people in need.
options.note: True and central to the parable, but it also pictures the saving mercy of Christ Himself.

options.text: He teaches that only Samaritans can be saved.
options.note: The Samaritan is the hero of mercy, not a claim about who alone is saved.

options.text: He proves that religious leaders are always right.
options.note: The priest and Levite pass by; the parable exposes religion without mercy.

questions: Jesus told the seventy-two to rejoice most that their names are written in heaven. When your week goes well or badly, where does your joy actually rest?
The lawyer wanted to know how little love he could get away with. Where are you tempted to draw a line around who counts as your neighbor?
A priest and a Levite saw the wounded man and passed by. Who is the person you are most tempted to cross to the other side of the road to avoid?
The Samaritan's mercy cost him time, oil, wine, money, and a promise to return. What would costly, not merely convenient, mercy look like for you this week?
Martha was worried and upset about many things, while Mary sat and listened. What good and busy things are crowding out your time at Jesus' feet?

translationNotes.note: Seventy-two follows many early manuscripts; others read seventy, as reflected in the BSB footnote. The number may echo the seventy or seventy-two nations of Genesis 10, hinting that the mission is ultimately for the whole world.
The lawyer weaves together Deuteronomy 6:5 and Leviticus 19:18. Jesus affirms this summary of the law, then presses its meaning home through the parable.
Two denarii represented roughly two days' wages for a laborer, enough for an extended stay at the inn; the Samaritan also pledges to cover any further cost, underscoring the generosity of his mercy.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Luke 10:1-42. Headings, line breaks, footnote markers, and poem markers were skipped when building verse text; em and en dashes were converted to commas or clean breaks. Sermon verified this session from the trusted cluster, Tim Keller on the Good Samaritan at gospelinlife.com.
